AktState_explanation: The target 'would have been better' denotes a durative attribute or state (comparative quality condition) that would hold over time without dynamic change, internal progression, or eventive unfolding, matching the set_to_1_if criteria for stative situations.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional paired with the protasis 'if that man had not been born', expressing a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in 'had not been born' negates the embedded clause, not the target gram 'would have been better'; the target gram itself expresses a positive counterfactual state without direct negation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'would have been better' contains no overt lexical or phrasal past time adverbial; the 'if' clause is conditional, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The clause is irrealis—it expresses a counterfactual conditional about an unrealized, hypothetical situation ('would have been better if...'), not an actual event in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is irrealis (counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, unrealized situation) and does not present an actual event or resulting state in the reference world.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause is irrealis (counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized, hypothetical situation) and describes a state transition rather than a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' expresses a counterfactual stative predicate (better = state/quality) without a construed transition or bounded occurrence; it describes an unrealized state rather than a completed bounded event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with 'have been', presenting a counterfactual/conditional evaluation of a hypothetical situation rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'better' (non-verbal predicate), not a past participle verb; 'been' functions as a lexical copula, not a passive auxiliary.

FullText: » It is written that the Son of man will depart . The man who betrays him will experience much grief . It would have been better if that man had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target is morphologically passive ('had not been born'), and the lexical verb 'bear/birth' licenses both an external argument (the agent who would give birth) and an internal argument (the patient—the man being born), making it the passive of a transitive verb with a recoverable patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'be born' is a change-of-state event where the sole argument (that man) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the event of coming into existence, and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a change-of-state/coming-into-being event where the sole argument (that man) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a punctual boundary transition marking entry into existence, lacking a durative process phase and culminating in an instantaneous change-of-state rather than a result state encoded in the predicate's meaning.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a punctual transition into existence/a new state of being, naturally modified by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'born at midnight'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a single punctual event marking the onset of existence with a defined result state (the state of being born/alive), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had not been born' denotes a change-of-state event marking the onset of existence; birth is a bounded instantaneous event with an inherent endpoint, not a durative state that holds over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if that man had not been born'), expressing a contrary-to-fact condition paired with the apodosis 'It would have been better', which uses the modal perfect 'would have been'.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is negated with 'not' in 'had not been born', directly negating the eventuality of being born according to the first set_to_1_if criterion.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'had not been born' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the clause is embedded in a conditional structure ('if that man had not been born') which does not establish a specific temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The clause is irrealis (counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, unrealized situation introduced by 'would have been better if'), which sets it to 0 according to the CRIs.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is irrealis (counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, unrealized situation) and negates the event; no actual transition to a result state is entailed in the reference world.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause is irrealis (counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, unrealized situation) and describes a transition into non-existence rather than a continuous state holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' is negated with 'not', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time, leaving the situation open rather than presenting it as a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('It would have been better if that man had not been born'), expressing a hypothetical contrary-to-fact situation rather than an asserted reality.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had not been born' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive marking, which satisfies the set_to_1_if criteria.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not been born' instantiates passive voice morphology (auxiliary 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'born' of transitive verb 'bear'), with the grammatical subject 'that man' functioning as the semantic patient (the entity brought into existence), meeting the set_to_1_if criteria.

FullText: As he spoke , Judas , one of the twelve , followed by a crowd carrying swords and spears , approached him . The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The chief priest and elders of the people') and an overt direct object noun phrase ('them'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had sent' licenses an external argument (the chief priest and elders as agent) and introduces a direct object ('them'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had sent' licenses an internal argument ('them'), which is a direct object pronoun functioning as the theme of the sending event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a durative process of dispatching/sending that culminates in a defined result state (them being sent/dispatched), with completion encoded in the predicate meaning.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the act of dispatching/sending), which is a definable threshold event that can occur at a specific temporal boundary and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a bounded, completed action with a defined result state (the sending was accomplished), not a dynamic durative situation without a defined goal.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a bounded dynamic event (sending) involving change-of-state and internal eventive unfolding, which marks a specific past action with a clear endpoint, not a durative state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the sending occurred before the approaching), describing an event that actually occurred in the story world, without any indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had sent' is not negated; it expresses a realized past event with no negator ('not', 'n't', negative quantifier, or 'never') directly modifying it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had sent' entails a change of agency/causation where the chief priest and elders initiated the sending of the crowd, and this resulting state (the crowd being sent/dispatched) holds at the reference time as the basis for their approach to Jesus; the focus is on the outcome state rather than on the occurrence as a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had sent' introduces a change of possession/agency where the chief priest and elders caused the crowd to be dispatched, and this resulting state (the crowd being sent and now approaching) persists at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a discrete bounded event (the act of sending) completed prior to the reference time, not a continuous situation holding throughout a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' presents a bounded, completed action (the sending of the crowd) as a single whole event prior to the narrative sequence, construing the transition of sending as accomplished.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had sent' asserts a realized past action in the actual world (the chief priests and elders had already sent the crowd), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, forming a past perfect construction ('had sent') rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'The chief priest and elders' is the semantic agent (doer) of the action, and 'them' is the semantic patient (object affected), making this active voice, not passive.

FullText: » This has taken place so that the writings of the prophets will come true . « Then all his disciples left him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has taken place' is an intransitive phrasal verb that does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), so it does not instantiate ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes an occurrence/happening event with no external argument licensed; the sole argument (the event itself, implicit) functions as an internal argument, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for existence, appearance, or occurrence without licensing an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb phrase 'has taken place' is an idiom denoting existence/occurrence of an event and does not license an external argument (agent/initiator); rather, the subject 'this' functions as an internal argument (theme), making it unaccusative and thus not ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous occurrence of an event without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it marks a boundary transition into existence rather than a culminating result state.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has taken place' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of occurrence/reality, can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'at that moment'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a bounded event with a defined result state (the occurrence of an event), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and is a single punctual event marking completion rather than an ongoing activity.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has taken place' denotes a bounded dynamic event with a clear endpoint and internal eventive unfolding (something occurring/happening), which instantiates change rather than a static state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has taken place' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world, without any indication of counterfactuality or conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has taken place' is not negated; the sentence is affirmative and describes an event that is realized, not absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has taken place' is not directly modified by any lexical or phrasal past time adverb; 'so that' is a purposive conjunction, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The clause focuses on a current state of affairs (the event has occurred and its purpose/result is now in effect) rather than on whether the event has occurred at least once in the subject's life history, and the purpose clause indicates the resulting state that holds at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'take place' is an activity/event verb that does not introduce a result state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports that an event occurred in the past without encoding a transition to a new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (something taking place) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the occurrence of the event itself, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has taken place' is a transition event (the occurrence of an event) construed as a completed bounded whole, presenting a single discrete occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' asserts a realized event in the actual world using the present perfect tense, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context modifying it.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'taken', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect pattern that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has taken place' is active voice where the grammatical subject 'This' is the semantic agent/experiencer, and 'take place' is an intransitive verb with no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle structure).

FullText: Then the high priest ripped his robes apart and said : » He has said evil against God ! Why do we need more witnesses ? You have heard his words of blasphemy ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'You' and an overt direct object noun phrase 'his words of blasphemy', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as the agent/experiencer) and introduces a direct object ('his words of blasphemy'), thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'have heard' licenses an internal argument ('his words of blasphemy'), which is a direct object complement of the verb, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual, instantaneous perceptual transition into awareness without a durative process phase leading to a defined goal or result state; hearing is construed as a single moment of perception rather than a measurable duration culminating in completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness of the blasphemous words, occurring at a definable temporal boundary, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of 'his words of blasphemy', which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing blasphemous words), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the witnesses did hear the blasphemous words), with no indication of counterfactuality or conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; it is a positive assertion in an exclamatory statement ('You have heard his words of blasphemy!').
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have heard' contains no overt lexical or phrasal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'Then' is a logical connector introducing reported speech, not a temporal anchor for the hearing event.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' presents the event of hearing as part of the addressees' life history (an experience that has occurred), focusing on whether the event happened rather than on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' is a bare perception event that reports a past auditory experience without introducing a result state that persists at the reference time; hearing words does not create a lasting change of state in the hearer.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (hearing the blasphemous words) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completed action of perceiving specific utterances, not on a state or activity extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' construes hearing as a bounded perceptual event with a completed occurrence (the act of perceiving the blasphemous words as a discrete whole), rather than as an ongoing state or process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' asserts a realized fact in the actual world—the witnesses have actually heard the blasphemous words—and contains no modal, conditional, future, or wish context.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'heard', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect pattern.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'You' is the semantic agent (the one who heard/perceived), and 'his words of blasphemy' is the semantic patient (the object heard); this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: He said : » I have done wrong in giving a righteous man to you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done wrong' lacks an overt direct object; 'wrong' functions as a predicative complement describing the action, not as an internal argument of the verb 'do'.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I' as agent), and the clause contains an overt direct object 'wrong' introduced by the target verb, satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'have done wrong' licenses an internal argument realized as the prepositional phrase 'in giving a righteous man to you', which functions as a complement specifying the nature of the wrongdoing, thus instantiating an internal argument structure that disqualifies ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'do wrong' denotes a punctual moral transgression or boundary crossing without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined completion state; the wrongness is a state entered instantaneously rather than built up through a measurable process.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have done wrong' denotes a durative action or state of wrongdoing rather than a punctual transition into a new result state; it describes an unfolding process of committing a wrong action, not an instantaneous boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have done wrong' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (the wrongdoing is finished), not a dynamic durative situation without a goal, and lacks the homogeneous, ongoing character required for AktAct.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have done wrong' denotes a bounded dynamic event (a change-of-state or wrongdoing action) with internal eventive unfolding and a clear endpoint, not a durative state that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have done wrong' presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (a factual past action), without any indication that it is contrary to fact or part of a counterfactual conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'wrong' is a predicate adjective describing the action, not a syntactic negator (not/n't/never/negative quantifier) that negates the auxiliary 'have', so the eventuality 'have done' is presented as realized, not absent.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have done wrong' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'have done wrong' presents the event as part of the subject's life history without asserting a resulting state; it answers whether the wrongdoing occurred, focusing on the experience itself rather than on a persisting consequence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have done wrong' is a bare action verb describing a past wrongdoing without introducing a persisting result state at the reference time; it reports a past action without a current binding or resultant condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (doing wrong) with focus on the transition into a wrongful state rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks any duration adverbial or habitual framing.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have done wrong' presents a bounded transition event (doing wrong = committing a wrongful act) construed as a completed whole, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events asserted as completed occurrences.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done wrong' is embedded in reported speech asserting a realized fact about a past action, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have done wrong' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'done', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect pattern that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' corresponds to the semantic agent (the doer of the wrongdoing), and 'have done' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'do'), not passive voice, as there is no 'been' auxiliary and no 'by'-phrase expressing an external agent.

FullText: While he was seated in judgment his wife sent a message to him . She said , » Do not judge that righteous man . I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have had' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'I' and an overt direct object noun phrase 'much trouble', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I' as agent/experiencer) and introduces a direct object 'much trouble', making it transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'have had' licenses an internal argument (direct object) 'much trouble', which satisfies the set_to_0_if condition requiring presence of an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have had trouble' denotes a state or condition (experiencing trouble/distress) that lacks an inherent defined goal or completion point; trouble is a durative state without a built-in endpoint encoded in the predicate itself.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have had much trouble' unfolds over a measurable duration (the entire day) rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and the durative process of experiencing trouble is the core meaning rather than a punctual transition into a new state.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have had trouble' denotes a bounded experiential state with a defined result (the trouble experienced), not a dynamic durative activity; it is a completed occurrence marked by the perfective aspect and temporal frame 'this day', not an ongoing homogeneous situation.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have had much trouble' denotes a bounded experiential event with internal development (the occurrence of troubling dreams), marking a change from a prior state of not having experienced this trouble, rather than a static condition that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him' presents a situation that actually occurred (the wife experienced trouble in a dream), with no indication of counterfactuality or conditional structure; it is a factual statement of past experience.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have had much trouble' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('Do not judge') applies to a different verb and does not negate the eventuality of having had trouble.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have had much trouble' is directly modified by the phrasal past time adverb 'this day', which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause focuses on the current state of trouble experienced 'this day in a dream' at reference time, not on whether the event has occurred at least once in the subject's life history; the duration adverbial 'this day' indicates an ongoing situation rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('this day') indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed transition to a result state that persists at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (trouble experienced during a dream) that occurred and is now completed, rather than a situation holding continuously up to the reference time; the focus is on the resultant state of having experienced trouble, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have had much trouble this day in a dream' denotes a stative situation (experiencing trouble/distress) without a construed boundary or transition; the perfect aspect here profiles the resultant state rather than a bounded completed event, and the duration phrase 'this day' makes the internal temporal span accessible.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have had much trouble' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (the wife reports a dream she actually experienced), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have had' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'had', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect pattern.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (experiencer) of the event, and 'trouble' is the grammatical object (semantic patient); this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'be/been' or 'get/got' introducing a past participle.

FullText: » What evil has he done ? « Pilate asked . But they shouted : » Impale him ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'done' introduces an overt internal argument 'evil' (direct object), satisfying the criterion for transitive verbs with overt grammatical subject and direct object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (he/the subject as agent), and the clause contains a direct object 'evil', satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'done' licenses an internal argument 'evil' (direct object), which violates the ASUnrg feature requirement of no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb phrase 'has done evil' denotes a durative process of committing harmful acts that culminates in a result state (evil having been perpetrated), with completion built into the meaning of the predicate, matching the pattern of 'I have done the work.'
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'do evil' denotes a punctual transition into a state of having committed wrongdoing, can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'What evil has he done today?'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'do evil' entails a defined result state (evil deeds completed) and the internal argument 'evil' is bounded/quantized, making this a telic accomplishment rather than an atelic activity.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done evil' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the completion of an evil action) involving change-of-state and eventive unfolding, which sets it to 0 rather than a durative state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'What evil has he done?' is a factual interrogative asking about events that actually occurred in the narrative world, not a counterfactual conditional expressing a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' is not negated; the negation ('What evil') is a question formation asking about evil that was done, not negating the doing itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have done evil' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'What evil has he done?' presents the event as part of the subject's life history, asking whether and what evil acts have occurred at least once, without asserting a resulting state—this aligns with the DtExp intuition and the perception/communication focus criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done evil' introduces a result state (evil deeds committed) that persists at the reference time, encoding a transition from a state of not having done evil to having done evil, with the resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'What evil has he done?' describes a discrete bounded event (the doing of evil) with focus on the completed action/result rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for punctual results and transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'What evil has he done?' presents a completed action as a bounded whole with a construed endpoint, construing the predicate 'do evil' as a transition event that has occurred, fitting the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done evil' appears in an interrogative clause ('What evil has he done?') that seeks confirmation of an actual fact—a realized state of affairs in the actual world—rather than projecting a hypothetical, future, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'done' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect pattern.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'he' corresponds to the semantic agent (doer) of the action, and 'evil' is the grammatical object corresponding to the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'be/been' or 'get/got' introducing a past participle.

FullText: About the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice , » Eli , Eli , lama sabachthani ? « Which translated means : » My God , my God , why have you forsaken me ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have forsaken' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object noun phrase ('me'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') that acts upon the internal argument ('me'), making this a transitive verb with an overt direct object, not an unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'have forsaken' licenses an internal argument (direct object 'me'), which satisfies the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb introduces an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a durative process of abandonment leading to a defined result state (being forsaken/abandoned), which is encoded in the predicate's meaning as the outcome that defines completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (abandonment/separation) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption, meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' denotes a defined result state (abandonment/rejection) that marks completion of a bounded action, not a dynamic durative activity without a goal.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a change-of-state (abandonment/separation) involving a bounded dynamic event with an internal endpoint, not a durative state that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' is a factual utterance expressing a situation that has actually occurred in the narrative world, not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have forsaken' is not negated; the negation 'why' is an interrogative adverb, not a negator that negates the eventuality of forsaking.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have forsaken' contains no overt lexical or phrasal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' entails a change of state (abandonment/withdrawal of support) that holds at the reference time, with focus on the resulting state of being forsaken rather than on the occurrence of the event as a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' is a communication verb reporting a past interaction (Jesus's utterance) without establishing a persisting binding state; it expresses a complaint about abandonment rather than a current state of being forsaken that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a transition into a state of abandonment (forsaking) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the resultant state of having been abandoned, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' construes the act of forsaking as a completed transition event (abandonment), presenting it as a bounded whole rather than an ongoing state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have forsaken' appears in a direct speech utterance asserting a realized state of affairs (abandonment) in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have forsaken' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (the one doing the forsaking), and 'me' is the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, despite the presence of 'have'.

FullText: He laid it in his own new tomb , which he had cut out of the rock . Then he rolled a great stone in front of the door of the tomb , and departed .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had cut out of' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'he' and an overt direct object 'it' (referring to the tomb), satisfying the condition for transitive verbs with both subject and object present.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'cut' licenses an external argument (the agent 'he' who performed the cutting action), and the clause contains a direct object ('it' referring to the tomb), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had cut' licenses an internal argument (direct object) realized as the head of a relative clause ('which' = the tomb that he cut out of the rock), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'cut out of' denotes a durative process of carving/excavating the rock that necessarily culminates in a defined result state (a completed tomb), with completion built into the predicate meaning.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'cut out of' denotes a durative process of carving/excavating stone rather than a punctual transition into a new state; the event unfolds over measurable duration and represents the culmination of a process phase rather than the boundary point itself.

FullText: The angel spoke to the women : » I know you seek Jesus who has been impaled ; do not fear .

AktState_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (the transition from not-impaled to impaled) marking a dynamic eventive boundary, not a static condition that holds without internal development.

FullText: » He is not here , for he has risen just as he promised . Come ; see the place where the Lord lay .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument initiating an action.

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a bounded event with a defined result state (the state of being risen/resurrected), not a dynamic durative activity without a goal.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (from dead to alive/resurrected) with a clear boundary and dynamic progression, which instantiates eventive rather than stative semantics.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has risen' presents a situation understood to have actually occurred in the actual world (a factual past event), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen' entails a change of location (from lying down to risen) with a resulting state that persists at reference time (he is currently risen/alive), which matches the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs encoding transitions into new physical states that remain in effect.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'has risen' describes a change of location, but the context 'He is not here' explicitly indicates the mover has not remained at a destination; instead, the clause reports a past event (resurrection) without entailing a persisting result state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to risen) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the completion of that transition event.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, presenting a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, matching the set_to_1_if criteria for transition events.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (He is not here because he has risen), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (the one performing the action of rising), not a passive construction with auxiliary 'be/been' or 'get/got' introducing a past participle.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a bounded change of state with a defined result state (being alive/resurrected), not a dynamic durative activity, and the completion of this state change is entailed in the predicate's meaning.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (transition from dead to alive) with a clear boundary marking the onset of a new condition, which constitutes dynamic eventive unfolding rather than a static state.

DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has risen' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' construes a transition event (rising from death) as a completed whole, presenting a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the reported speech, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context modifying it.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'told' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces a direct object ('you'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt grammatical objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'have told' licenses an internal argument (direct object 'you'), which is realized as the complement of the verb, thus failing the set_to_1 criteria.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a punctual speech act of communication that is completed at a single moment of utterance, lacking a durative process phase with an inherent goal or result state encoded in the predicate itself.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a punctual perceptual/cognitive transition into awareness (the disciples becoming aware of information), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('as I have told you at that moment'), and represents a single instantaneous act of communication that transitions the listener into a state of knowing, meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.

AktState_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a bounded perceptual/communicative event marking the completion of a prior speech act, which is eventive rather than a static state holding over time.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have told' is not negated; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) applies to it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have told' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have told' entails a change in the informational state of the addressee (now possessing knowledge previously communicated), and the focus is on the resulting state of the addressee's knowledge at reference time rather than on whether the telling event occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have told' is a communication verb that merely reports a past interaction (prior instruction) without establishing a persisting binding state; the clause does not entail that the telling itself remains in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' describes a discrete past communicative event (the telling) rather than a situation holding continuously up to the reference time; the focus is on the completed action of telling, not on a state or activity extending through time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' denotes a stative situation (knowing/having informed) without a construed boundary or transition; it expresses a resultant state rather than packaging the telling as a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have told' asserts a realized past action in the actual world ('as I have told you'), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have told' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'told', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (doer) of the telling, and 'you' is the semantic patient (object); this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: Jesus came to them and spoke to them , saying : » All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth .

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been given' is a present perfect passive asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (authority has been given to Jesus), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been given' includes 'been' followed by a past participle 'given', which instantiates a progressive perfect construction (present perfect passive), matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive marking with 'been'.

FullText: » Teach them to observe all the things I have commanded you . Behold , I am with you always , even to the end of the age . «

FullText: Jesus said : » The time has come . The kingdom of God is at hand . Repent and believe the good news . «

FullText: » What do you want with us Jesus of Nazareth ? Have you come to destroy us ? I know who you are . You are the Holy One of God ! «

AktState_explanation: The target 'have come (to destroy)' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of arrival with purposive intent, involving dynamic change and eventive unfolding rather than a static condition.
